column3: summary

The secretary of state's office instructed local election officials to inactivate 3,251 possible noncitizen registration records. The office identified these records after cross-referencing the voter list with noncitizen identification numbers from the U.S. Department of Homeland Security. The secretary acknowledged that it was possible some of these individuals had become naturalized citizens.
column4: inactivate 3,251 possible noncitizen||cross-referencing the voter list

column3: A noncitizen pleaded guilty to assuming the identity of a US citizen to obtain a passport, register to vote, and vote in multiple elections.

Update to the 8/13/2024 entry: A federal judge blocked the inactivation of 3,251 possible noncitizen registration records on the grounds that federal law prohibits systematic list maintenance within 90 days of a federal election.
Update to the 9/11/2024 entry: The U.S. Department of Justice sentenced an individual for voting as a noncitizen in multiple elections.

The secretary of state's office identified and directed the removal of 186 possible noncitizens on Alabama's voter list who did not provide documentary proof of citizenship. Of these individuals, 25 allegedly voted. The secretary's office used the SAVE database to identify these individuals but did not provide further information on methodology or timeframe.
column4: identified and directed the removal||did not provide further information

column3: The U.S. Department of Justice charged an individual with allegedly registering to vote and voting as a noncitizen in the 2022 and 2024 general elections.

State court records indicate one confirmed case of noncitizen voting since 2011. The individual was born in America Samoa and is a US national, but not a US citizen. The Alaska division of elections identified the individual after she won a local school board seat in 2023.

The state charged 10 individuals born in American Samoa with perjury and voter misconduct for voting or attempting to vote. Individuals born in American Samoa are US nationals, but are not US citizens and are therefore not eligible to vote.

The Alaska division of elections identified 70 possible noncitizens who have allegedly voted or attempted to vote since 2015. Additionally, the state's inactive voter list included 541 individuals who were flagged as possible noncitizens.
column4: allegedly voted or attempted to vote

column3: Arizona has required documentary proof of US citizenship to register to vote since 2004. However, some registration records do not have confirmed documentary proof of citizenship due to administrative and system issues. The issues impact voters who obtained a driver's license before 1996 and registered to vote after 2004. These individuals are not confirmed or suspected noncitizens. Rather, these 218,000 voters were marked as having provided proof of US citizenship when it is not confirmed whether they did or not. The uncertainty is due to the way the motor vehicle division categorized driver's licenses.

Update to the 10/3/2024 entry: Pinal County suspended the registration records of individuals whose citizenship was unconfirmed due to administrative and system issues and who later tried to update their registration without providing documentary proof of citizenship. Since October 9, Pinal County officials have suspended 1,106 such records.

Update to the 10/3/2024 entry: Maricopa County officials reviewed the records of 61,681 voters whose citizenship was previously unconfirmed due to administrative and system issues using the SAVE database. The review identified 137 possible noncitizens, 60 of whom allegedly voted in past elections.
Update to the 2/13/2026 entry: The Maricopa County recorder's office referred 137 possible noncitizens, along with an additional 70 individuals, to county prosecutors for investigation.
The Arkansas attorney general announced three noncitizens were arrested and charged with illegally voting. Two individuals indicated they were citizens on their voter registration forms.

The secretary of state announced there are roughly 240 possible noncitizens on Arkansas' voter list. Originally, the SAVE database identified 300+ possible noncitizens, but some were later identified as naturalized citizens whose information had not yet been updated in the SAVE database. Of the roughly 240 possible noncitizens, 30-40 individuals are believed to have voted in an election.

Update to the 10/2/2025 and 10/30/2025 entries: After federal and state officials reviewed Arkansas' voter list for possible noncitizens, two individuals were convicted of illegally voting. For one individual, the convictions were reportedly unrelated to immigration status.
The U.S. Department of Justice sentenced an individual for voting as a noncitizen in multiple elections over 20 years. The individual fraudulently used the identity of a US citizen.
A noncitizen pleaded guilty to illegally registering to vote and voting in the 2016 primary and general elections.
Orange County identified and removed 17 noncitizens from their voter list over a five-year period. All but one individual self-reported their noncitizen status.

A complaint sent to Connecticut's elections enforcement commission alleged that a Bridgeport city councilman convinced a noncitizen to register to vote and apply for an absentee ballot, which the councilman then allegedly cast on her behalf in the 2023 Democratic primary.

The state election crime unit arrested an individual on two counts of illegally voting. The individual allegedly voted in two local elections as a noncitizen after registering to vote using a counterfeit birth certificate.
Florida law enforcement charged an individual for falsely affirming US citizenship on voter registration applications and voting illegally as a noncitizen in two local elections.

The secretary of state requested citizenship data from U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services to verify citizenship status for seven individuals on Florida's voter list. The secretary noted that 144 noncitizens were found on the state's voter list last month and two noncitizens were arrested for voting illegally in the last two years.

Florida law enforcement charged a noncitizen with allegedly voting in six local and national elections from 2022 to 2024. The individual allegedly falsely claimed US citizenship on his voter registration application in 2000.
The U.S. Department of Justice charged two noncitizens with allegedly registering to vote and voting in the 2024 general election.
The U.S. Department of Justice charged an individual with falsely claiming citizenship to register to vote and vote in the 2024 presidential primary.
The U.S. Department of Justice indicted an individual for falsely affirming US citizenship and voting illegally as a noncitizen in the 2020 election.

Charlotte County identified 15 possible noncitizens from 176,000 registration records uploaded to the SAVE database. Of these, three were mistakenly added to the county's voter list and two were naturalized citizens.

The Florida office of election crimes and security investigated the citizenship status of 835 individuals. The review identified at least 198 possible noncitizens who registered to vote or voted. Of these, 170 were referred to law enforcement. The remaining 28 individuals were referred to the elections division for list maintenance.

Of the 162,600 registered voters in Alachua County, officials have removed nine possible noncitizens since January 2025, three of whom have voted. Of the 444,900 registered voters in Polk County, officials have removed 69 possible noncitizens over the last year. These individuals were all identified through the SAVE database.
Update to the 5/2/2025 and 11/4/2025 entries: Two individuals pleaded guilty to falsely affirming US citizenship and voting illegally as noncitizens.

Georgia conducted the first citizenship audit of its voter list, placing 2,258 individuals under "pending citizenship status." These individuals were not added to the state's voter rolls and have never voted.||The audit found that citizenship could not be verified through the SAVE database for 1,634 individuals who had attempted to register to vote. The Department of Driver Services flagged another 624 individuals as possible noncitizens.

Georgia conducted a citizenship audit of its voter list, identifying 20 noncitizens who were then removed from the rolls and referred to local prosecutors for potential criminal charges. Nine of the 20 noncitizens had previously voted. These individuals were identified because they self-reported their noncitizen status to excuse themselves from jury duty. The state opened case files for another 156 individuals that required more information to confirm citizenship.

The secretary of state's office identified 36 possible noncitizens on Idaho's voter list for removal. Additionally, Ada County has removed 78 noncitizens from its voter list since 2020. Ada County records show one instance of noncitizen voting since 2020.
The secretary of state announced the arrest of an individual charged with registering and voting as a noncitizen.

Roughly a dozen possible noncitizens were referred for possible criminal charges out of 760 possible noncitizens initially flagged by the secretary of state. These 760 individuals were identified through the SAVE database.

An error with Illinois' automatic voter registration system resulted in 545 noncitizens registering to vote despite self-reporting their noncitizen status.||The state board of elections updated the number of possible noncitizens who voted in the 2018 and 2019 elections to 12 individuals, down from initial reports of 16 individuals.

The secretary of state referred a case of alleged noncitizen voting to law enforcement. The individual allegedly voted in multiple elections and was identified when he submitted a foreign passport as proof of citizenship for temporary credentials issued by the bureau of motor vehicles.
column4: referred a case of alleged noncitizen voting

column3: The attorney general and secretary of state identified 165 possible noncitizens who were registered to vote after gaining access to the SAVE database. Of these individuals, at least 21 are alleged to have voted in recent elections.

The secretary of state identified 2,176 possible noncitizens who allegedly voted or registered to vote. Of these, 2,022 individuals had self-reported their noncitizen status before voting or registering to vote.

Update to the 10/22/2024 entry: The secretary of state reduced the number of possible noncitizens identified as having voted or registered to vote from 2,176 to 277 after comparing state data with the SAVE database. Of the 277, 35 noncitizens voted in Iowa last year and another five noncitizens had their ballots rejected.
column4: reduced the number of possible noncitizens||voted in Iowa list year

column3: A noncitizen in Palo Alto County was convicted of illegally voting in local elections four years ago.

A Hancock County jury acquitted an individual accused of illegally voting as a noncitizen. The state has filed charged against at least five other individuals who are accused of illegally registering and/or voting as noncitizens in Iowa since 2023.
A Johnson County jury acquitted an individual accused of illegally registering to vote and voting as a noncitizen.

Update to the 9/18/2025 entry: Of 2,176 possible noncitizens on the state's voter rolls, the state attorney general charged six individuals with first-degree election misconduct. Two were acquitted, two were convicted, and two have pending charges. Of the convictions, one was given a deferred judgement and probation and the other was later ruled to be entitled to a new trial.

The secretary of state announced the conviction of an individual who voted as a noncitizen in multiple elections. The conviction is the first related to noncitizen voting since the secretary gained the authority to prosecute voter fraud in 2015.
The attorney general filed charges against an individual who allegedly voted multiples times as a noncitizen.

A Kansas mayor was convicted of illegally voting as a noncitizen. The individual said he did not know voting as a permanent US resident was illegal and pleaded guilty to three misdemeanor counts of disorderly election conduct.
The secretary of state reported 48 noncitizens have been removed from Louisiana's voter list since 2022, but could not confirm whether any of these individuals voted.

The secretary of state announced that the office identified 390 possible noncitizen registered voters, 79 of whom allegedly voted in at least one election. These alleged cases were identified through an investigation with the SAVE database and the FBI analyzing registration records spanning back to the 1980s.

Update to the 9/4/2025 entry: The secretary of state announced that the office identified 403 possible noncitizens registered to vote, 83 of whom allegedly voted in at least one election since the 1980s. These individuals were identified through the SAVE database.

In December 2024, the Maine Wire updated the number of possible noncitizens it had identified as registered voters from six possible noncitizens in October 2024 to 10 possible noncitizens. Of the 10 possible noncitizens, eight have reportedly voted since 2016. The Maine Wire identified these individuals by analyzing a subset of MaineCare records and voter data from the voter registration database system.
column4: updated the number of possible noncitizens

column3: A possible noncitizen was charged with illegally voting in the 2018 gubernatorial election. The individual allegedly registered to vote in 1996.
A noncitizen arrested by federal immigration officials in Iowa allegedly has a registration record in Maryland. The individual had previously worked in Maryland.

According to a report from the Public Interest Legal Foundation, the Boston election department disclosed 70 recent examples of registered noncitizens who were removed from the city's voter list, 22 of whom have voted. These noncitizens self-reported their citizenship status as part of the state's annual census.
column4: report from the||self-reported their citizenship status

column3: The Michigan department of state reported 16 possible cases of noncitizen voting out of 5.7 million ballots cast in the 2024 general election. Thirteen cases were referred to the state attorney general, one case is still being investigated by the department of state, and one individual is now deceased.

The U.S. Department of Justice filed a criminal complaint against a noncitizen for allegedly registering to vote and voting in the 2024 general election. The noncitizen was a student who allegedly registered using a student ID.

An individual pleaded guilty to illegally voting as a noncitizen. The individual fraudulently assumed the identity of a Puerto Rican man to apply for a Michigan driver's license and was registered to vote at the same time.

The Macomb County clerk claimed to have identified 15 possible noncitizens on the county's voter list, three of whom have allegedly voted. However, the Michigan bureau of elections reviewed these allegations and found that at least three of these 15 individuals were US citizens and another four individuals were previously already from the voter list.
column4: claimed to have identified||reviewed these allegations

column3: A review of every prosecution related to noncitizen registration and voting in Minnesota since 2015 identified three convictions.
column4: review of every prosecution||identified three convictions

column3: A noncitizen was charged with allegedly registering to vote and voting in a February 2025 local election. Investigators found the noncitizen had self-reported their citizenship status on the application.
An individual was charged with illegally voting as a noncitizen in the 2024 general election after registering to vote in 2023.

The secretary of state announced that Jackson County officials removed a noncitizen who allegedly voted in the 2024 general election from their voter list. It is unclear if criminal charges were filed.

The Phillips County attorney's office charged two noncitizens with allegedly registering to vote and voting in an October 2021 municipal elections. The county clerk and recorder's office cancelled these registrations in January 2022.

Update to the 2/7/2022 entry: Two noncitizens charged in February 2022 with allegedly registering to vote failed to appear in court. The case was vacated and referred to federal immigration officials.
The secretary of state's office identified 23 possible noncitizens on the state's voter list through the SAVE database. Several of these individuals had allegedly voted in multiple elections.

The secretary of state's office identified over 100 noncitizens who had provided evidence of noncitizenship to the department of motor vehicles and completed a voter registration application. Of these individuals, 21 have allegedly voted.

The secretary of state received an elections integrity violation report alleging noncitizen voting in 2020. The secretary's office then conducted a review of all records of individuals who presented an immigration document when obtaining a driver's license. However, the office ultimately determined that generalized information from the DMV could not serve as a basis for an investigation as many voters were likely naturalized at the time of, or after their interaction with the DMV.

The Trump campaign and state and national Republicans filed a lawsuit claiming thousands of noncitizens are registered to vote and have voted in Nevada. The data for these claims come from a previous lawsuit, which was dismissed.

The U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation closed an investigation into voter fraud in Nevada in 2020. An FBI review of the state's voter list revealed fewer than 40 potential instances of noncitizen voting.

The New Hampshire justice department charged a noncitizen with three felonies for allegedly voting in three different elections in 2023 and 2024. The individual allegedly registered to vote by signing an affidavit attesting they were a US citizen.

The Public Interest Legal Foundation reportedly identified 616 noncitizens who were "engaged on some level" with New Jersey's voter registration system, 76% of whom had self-reported their citizenship status. The review was based on data disclosed by several counties.

The U.S. Department of Justice charged two individuals with registering to vote and voting in the 2020 general election. The individuals allegedly falsely certified and attested to being US citizens on their registration forms.
The U.S. Department of Justice charged an individual with illegally voting as a noncitizen in the 2020 election.

The North Carolina state board of elections' 2016 post-election audit identified 41 noncitizens who had voted. The audit also identified an additional 34 voters who later provided documentary proof of citizenship and were confirmed to be US citizens.

The North Carolina state board of elections identified nine registered voters as possible noncitizens and is investigating their citizenship status. These individuals were identified due to a new law requiring state election officials to work with court officials to identify voters who request to be excused from jury duty based on citizenship.
column4: identified nine registered voters||due to a new law

column3: A federal grand jury indicted an individual for allegedly registering to vote and voting in the 2022 and 2024 general elections as a noncitizen.
The US attorney for the western district of North Carolina claimed the state's division of motor vehicles allegedly illegally registered six noncitizens to vote, seemingly without their knowledge.
column4: claimed the state's division of motor vehicles

column3: The secretary of state referred 11 individuals who provided documentation of noncitizenship for further investigation of potential election crimes. Of these 11 individuals, 10 had registration records and one may have voted illegally.
A review of allegations of noncitizen voting identified one individual charged with voter fraud out of 521 cases.
The secretary of state referred evidence of 597 possible noncitizen registration records to the state attorney general, including 138 individuals who appear to have voted in an election.
Of the 621 criminal referrals of voter fraud sent to the state attorney general, prosecutors indicted nine individuals for voting as noncitizens over the span of 10 years.
The secretary of state's public integrity division and office of data analytics and archives identified 62 registration records belonging to possible noncitizens.

The secretary of state announced that the office's election integrity unit identified 78 possible noncitizens on Ohio's voter list. Of these, nine had allegedly voted in an election. The secretary is referring these cases for potential prosecution.
column4: election integrity unit identified||referring these cases

column3: The secretary of state announced that the office's election integrity unit identified 30 possible noncitizens on Ohio's voter list. The review also identified 11 possible noncitizens from other states who appear to have voted in multiple jurisdictions. The secretary is referring these cases for potential prosecution.
The secretary of state announced the office identified 1,084 possible noncitizens who appear to have registered to vote. Of these, 167 allegedly voted in a federal election between 2018 and 2024.
The state election board identified five cases of possible noncitizen voter registration during the 2024 general election.

State officials identified 306 individuals on Oregon's voter list who were noncitizens at the time of registration, including two who allegedly voted. These individuals were registered due to clerical errors in marking the type of documentation presented when receiving or renewing a driver's license.

Update to the 9/13/2024 entry: 1,259 individuals who did not provide documentary proof of US citizenship at the time of registration have been registered to vote in error since 2021. Of these, 306 presented foreign passports during driver’s license renewal or application and were incorrectly marked as US citizens, and 953 were registered after presenting foreign birth certificates.||Of the 1,259 individuals, 10 allegedly voted, one of which became a naturalized citizen prior to voting.
column4: did not provide documentary proof of US citizenship||allegedly voted

column3: Update to the 9/13/2024 entry: The driver and motor vehicles services division identified an additional 302 individuals who were mistakenly registered to vote, including citizens of US territories. Of these, 178 were US nationals from American Samoa and Swains Island who were misclassified as US citizens, and 123 resulted from the same clerical error identified in September.

Update to the 9/23/2024 entry: Of the 10 individuals who had a voting history and were registered without providing documentary proof of citizenship at the time of registration, five were confirmed to be US citizens.

The driver and motor vehicles services division identified an additional 56 individuals who were mistakenly registered to vote. Fifty-four of these records were identified through a re-review of transactions with the driver and motor vehicles division.

Update to the 9/23/2024 entry: The secretary of state’s office forwarded three cases of possible noncitizen voting to the U.S. Department of Justice. Seven more individuals who may have voted illegally after they were registered in error remain under investigation.
column4: three cases of possible noncitizen voting

column3: The driver and motor vehicles division identified 118 individuals whose citizenship was initially established, but documents presented later on indicate noncitizenship. Most of these instances occurred between 2010 and 2020 due to clerical errors.||Of the 118 individuals, at least 13 allegedly had a voting history. The secretary of state's office is reviewing their records to determine if they became naturalized citizens before voting.
column4: identified 118 individuals||due to clerical errors||allegedly had a voting history

column3: Update to the 10/7/2024 entry: Of the nearly 200 American Samoan residents who were registered to vote in error, 10 voted in an election. Officials determined the 10 noncitizens had not intended to break the law.

Update to the 2/10/2025 entry: State officials shelved investigations into three cases of alleged noncitizen voting after a state attorney cautioned that the cases were "vulnerable" to claims of selective prosecution.

Pennsylvania election officials said that 544 ballots out of 93 million cast over 18 years may have been cast by possible noncitizens. These ballots were allegedly cast by noncitizens who later reported themselves as having mistakenly registered.
The Brennan Center identified 30 cases of possible noncitizen voting out of 23.5 million votes cast across 42 jurisdictions in the November 2016 election.
The Public Interest Legal Foundation claimed that Pennsylvania identified 1,160 individuals who self-reported their noncitizen status and requested cancellation of their voter registration.
The U.S. Department of Justice charged an individual with illegally voting in the 2024 general election as a noncitizen. The individual has allegedly voted in several past elections.

One noncitizen was identified as having been allowed to register to vote following a review of over 200,000 voter applications from the state's department of transportation. The noncitizen notified the agency at the time and was not registered to vote.
The South Carolina election commission said six possible noncitizens registered to vote over a four-year period due to human error.
column4: six possible noncitizens registered to vote

column3: South Dakota removed 273 possible noncitizens from the voter list after they were registered due to human error in marking citizenship status.

More than 14,000 registered voters were sent letters requesting documentary proof of US citizenship. The list was generated by comparing the voter list with data from the Tennessee department of safety and homeland security.

The secretary of state announced that 42 possible noncitizens identified through the SAVE database had allegedly voted in a past election and referred the case to the U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation for further review.
A noncitizen was convicted of illegally voting in Texas. The individual reportedly believed she was eligible to vote as a green card holder.

The secretary of state announced there are roughly 95,000 individuals with registration records whom the department of safety identified as noncitizens. Of those individuals, roughly 58,000 allegedly had a voting history.

The secretary of state ended the review of the possible noncitizens identified in January after agreeing to a settlement. The review had not accounted for individuals who later became naturalized citizens. One official acknowledged that the secretary of state's office had overstated the number of flagged voters by roughly 25,000 names as a result. Local officials also noted that they had already verified hundreds of flagged individuals were naturalized citizens.

An audit of the 2020 general election in Collin, Dallas, Harris, and Tarrant counties identified 74 possible noncitizens from cancelled registration records using data from local and court authorities.||Beginning in September 2021, the secretary of state implemented a process to compared the voter list against department of public safety citizenship data. From these comparisons, 11,737 possible noncitizens were identified statewide.||Counties later cancelled 278 registration records after confirming noncitizenship and 2,049 registration records after individuals failed to respond to requests for documentary proof of US citizenship.

The governor announced that Texas has removed more than 6,500 individuals identified as noncitizens from the voter list since the passage of SB 1 in 2021. Of those individuals, roughly 1,930 had allegedly voted.

Update to the 8/26/2024 entry: The governor revised an August press release claiming that more than 6,500 noncitizens had been removed from the voter list to describe the individuals as "potential" noncitizens. An investigation by ProPublica, The Texas Tribune, and Votebeat found that according to an August report from secretary of state's office, 581 possible noncitizens had been identified. The larger 6,500 number also included individuals who were removed after failing to receive or respond to notices that their citizenship was in question. Of the more than 70 individuals identified as possible noncitizens that news organizations have contacted, at least nine have so far been identified as US citizens.
column4: claiming that more than 6,500 noncitizens had been removed

column3: The secretary of state announced the office identified 33 possible noncitizens who allegedly voted in the November 2024 election using the SAVE database and referred these individuals to the attorney general for investigation.

The attorney general announced an investigation into more than 100 possible noncitizens who allegedly cast over 200 ballots during the 2020 and 2022 election cycles. These ballots represent roughly one-thousandth of one percent of the ballots cast in those elections.
column4: investigation into more than 100 possible noncitizens

column3: The secretary of state announced the office identified 2,724 possible noncitizen registration records after comparing Texas' voter list against the SAVE database.

Update to the 10/20/2025 entry: At least six individuals in three counties who were identified as possible noncitizens later confirmed their US citizenship. The secretary of state’s office additionally said counties have confirmed citizenship for “a handful” of voters.||Of 565 possible noncitizens flagged across seven counties, at least 193 individuals had submitted their applications through the Texas department of public safety, which requires documentary proof of US citizenship to register to vote. County officials had also registered 94 individuals who self-reported noncitizenship on their registration applications in error; none of these individuals cast ballots.||Officials in two counties identified one noncitizen each, neither of whom had voted in an election.

Update to the 10/20/2025 entry: Votebeat contacted 24 counties regarding investigations into voters flagged as possible noncitizens by the secretary of state’s office in October. Responding counties have confirmed the US citizenship of 16 flagged voters so far.

Update to the 10/20/2025 entry: Of 84 possible noncitizens flagged in Denton County, 14 later confirmed their US citizenship and 14 more were incorrectly registered due to a county error after self-reporting noncitizenship on their registration forms. None of these individuals had cast a ballot.||The remaining 56 flagged registration records were canceled after officials did not receive a response to the notice requiring documentary proof of citizenship. However, NPR identified at least one US citizen in Denton County who was mistakenly flagged as a possible noncitizen and subsequently had his voter registration canceled.

Update to the 10/20/2025 entry: Of the local officials who have responded to Votebeat's inquiries, at least 33 flagged individuals have been confirmed as US citizens. In Travis County, at least 11 of the 97 individuals identified as possible noncitizens had provided documentary proof of US citizenship to the Texas department of public safety.||Officials noted that some individuals who registered through the department of public safety had provided documentary proof of citizenship while others had not.
A possible noncitizen was accused of voting in the 2024 general election. The individual cancelled his voter registration after he was contacted by officials.

The U.S. Department of Justice charged an individual with illegally registering to vote and voting as a noncitizen. The individual allegedly committed identity theft, using stolen identity to register and vote.
column4: illegally registering to vote and voting

column3: Two local officials reported one instance of noncitizen registration in each of their counties in an interview with the Brennan Center. It is unclear when these instances occurred.

A state representative claimed to have identified at least two possible noncitizens who were registered to vote or had voted in Utah elections through the state's voter database. The representative also claimed that a third individual had been told he could register to vote despite not being a US citizen, though he ultimately did not register.

The lieutenant governor's office worked with the driver license division to conduct a citizenship review of Utah's voter list containing 2,069,640 registration records. Officials ultimately removed one possible noncitizen who had never voted and sent letters to 486 individuals requesting additional documentation to confirm their eligibility.

Between September 1, 2018 and August 31, 2019, the state cancelled 524 registration records belonging to individuals who self-reported noncitizenship during a transaction with the department of motor vehicles. Additionally, the department of motor vehicles is in the process of correcting some noncitizen reports after a limited number of erroneous registration records were included in the list due to a restructuring of internal processes at the agency.

Between September 1, 2019 and August 31, 2020, the state cancelled 802 registration records belonging to individuals who self-reported noncitizenship during a transaction with the department of motor vehicles.

In an executive order directing officials to cancel registration records of individuals who did not verify their citizenship to the department of motor vehicles, the governor stated that the state had identified and removed 6,303 such records between January 2022 and July 2024.

Update to the 8/7/2024 entry: A Washington Post review of state court records and interviews with election officials found no evidence of voting or attempted voting by noncitizens between January 2022 and July 2024.  Most of the 6,303 registration records identified as belonging to possible noncitizens from the voter list originated from paperwork errors at the Virginia department of motor vehicles.
column4: no evidence of voting or attempted voting

column3: A Norfolk official noted that there has only been one case of noncitizen voting in the last 15 years.

In Prince William County, officials removed 162 individuals between May 2023 and February 2024 that the state had flagged as possible noncitizens. Of these, 43 had voted between 2012 and 2020. However, all 43 had been registered as US citizens, affirmed their citizenship on earlier records, and had valid Social Security numbers.
column4: removed 162 individuals||had been registered as US citizens

column3: Officials in four jurisdictions removed 207 registration records belonging to possible noncitizens as part of an August executive order from the governor. Fourteen of these individuals later affirmed their citizenship.

A district court found that at least some of 1,600 individuals flagged as possible noncitizens and removed from the state voter list since August 7 are US citizens. The district court ordered the state to reinstate the voters, but the order was later blocked by the U.S. Supreme Court. It is unclear how many of the 1,600 individuals are US citizens.

RadioIQ's review of more than 650 cases of election-related crimes in the state court system found one instance of a possible noncitizen registering to vote and no confirmed instances of noncitizen voting over the last 20 years.
The secretary of state noted that there have been just 15 cases of noncitizen voting in the state between 1982 and 2025, accounting for 0.000006% of ballots cast.

The secretary of state's office reported that there has been one successful conviction for noncitizen voting since 2017. The individual was convicted after registering to vote and voting in the 2016 general election.
A report published by the state elections commission identified one suspected case of election fraud involving a possible noncitizen between February 2017 and February 2018.
A report published by the state elections commission identified one suspected case of election fraud involving a possible noncitizen between February 2018 and February 2019.
A report published by the state elections commission identified two suspected cases of election fraud involving a possible noncitizen between June 2022 and June 2023.
The state election commission said there have been three noncitizens referred for prosecution for voting illegally since 2019.
A report published by the state elections commission identified one suspected case of election fraud involving a possible noncitizen between September 2024 and November 2025.
An investigation into potential voting irregularities in Laramie County in 2024 determined that all 13 individuals who were initially flagged due to citizenship concerns voted legally.

The SAVE database identified three active registered voters as possible noncitizens. The secretary of state's office removed these individuals from the voter list and referred the cases to law enforcement for further investigation.

The secretary of state notified Johnson County of a possible noncitizen on the county's voter list identified through the SAVE database. The secretary referred the individual to law enforcement for further investigation of potential false voting.
